title: Present Value Calculator (PV of Future Money)
shortDescription: Calculate the present value of a future amount discounted at a given rate.
description: Find out what a future sum of money is worth today. Enter the future value, an annual discount rate and the number of years, and this calculator returns the present value using PV = future_value / (1 + rate/100)^years.
metaDescription: Free present value calculator. Discount a future amount to today's value using an annual rate and number of years. Instant PV result for finance and investing.

result.label: Present value
result.empty: Enter a future value, an annual rate and the number of years to see the present value.

sections.heading: What is present value?
paragraphs: Present value (PV) is what a future amount of money is worth today, given a discount rate. Money available now is worth more than the same amount later because it can be invested and earn returns.
The formula is PV = future_value / (1 + rate/100)^years. A higher rate or a longer time horizon both lower the present value.

sections.heading: Why present value matters
paragraphs: Present value lets you compare cash flows that arrive at different times on equal footing. It is the foundation of discounted cash flow analysis, bond pricing and investment decisions.
Use it to decide whether a future payout is worth a cost today, or to value an investment that pays off years from now.

howTo.name: Enter the future value
howTo.text: Type the amount of money you expect to receive in the future.

howTo.name: Set the annual rate
howTo.text: Enter the annual discount rate as a percentage, for example 5 for 5%.

howTo.name: Enter the number of years
howTo.text: Type how many years until you receive the money to get the present value.

faq.a: Present value is the current worth of a future sum of money, discounted at a chosen annual rate.
Use the return you could otherwise earn, or your cost of capital. Common choices range from 3% to 10%.

faq.q: Does a higher rate increase or decrease PV?
faq.a: A higher discount rate lowers the present value, because future money is discounted more heavily.
Yes. You can enter decimal years, such as 2.5, and the calculator handles it correctly.
